content.full_text:
Re:
Farn PolrrrcAr- PRACTTcES CoMMrssroN
P.O. Box 807 t 428 J Street r Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 e Fax (916) 322-0886
September 25,2001
Douglas Sherman
Post Office Box 1195
Desert Hot Springs, CA 92240
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-01-209
Dear Mr. Sherman:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 This letter is limited solely to
provisions of the Act, and should not be taken as advice or an opinion regarding any
other areas ofthe law potentially raised by your letter. This advice is based upon the facts
as provided in your request letter.2 Please note that this letter should not be construed to
evaluate any conduct that has already taken place. (Regulation 18329(bX8)(A) )
QUESTTON
As a candidate for city council, can you still provide printing goods and services
to the city?3
CONCLUSION
You can still provide printing goods and services to the city during your
candidacy because you are not a "public official" under the Act.
1 Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commissionregulations appear at Title 2, sections
I 8 1 0 9- 1 8 997, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 This advice is applicable and confers immunity only to tlre extent tlat the facts provided to us are
correct, and that all of the material facts have been disclosed. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71;
$ 83114.)
3 You have also inquired as to whether you can provide such goods and services to the city if you
are elected. You have not provided adequate information to respond to this query and it presents a purely
hlpothetical issue. As such, we decline to provide advice in that regard. (Reg. 1S329O)(8)(C)(D) )
:


File No. A-01-209
Page No. 2
FACTS
You are a candidate for Desert Hot Springs City Council.a Prior to running for
the council, the City of Desert Hot Springs was a client of your company. You furnished
business cards, letterhead, envelopes and forms to the city.
ANALYSIS
The Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in making or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest. ($ 87100.) Pursuant to Regulation 18700, an eight-
step analysis is applied to determine whether a public official has a conflict of interest in
a gtyen governmental decision.
Step One: Is the individual a "public official?"
S 82048 states:
"Public official" means every member, officer, employee or consultant
of a state or local government agency, but does not include judges and
court commissioners in the judicial branch of government. "Public
official" also does not include members of the Board of Governors and
designated employees of the State Bar of California, members of the
Judicial Council, and members of the Commission on Judicial
Performance, provided that they are subject to the provisions of Article
2.5 (commencing with Section 6035) of Chapter 4 of Division 3 of the
Business and Professions Code as provided in Section 6038 of that
article.
As previously advised, a mere candidate for public office is not a "public official"
under the above definition. (Knight Advice Letter, No. I-98-183.) As such, you are not
subject to the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act and may continue the above
described business relationship with the city during the pendency of your candidacy.5
a You have not provided any information to the effect that you are currenfly an official with the
city, or otherwise employed by it. ft is assumed for purposes of this letter that you are employed or self-
employed in a private business.
s Because you are not a "public official" under the Act, it is urmecessary to further analyze your
request under the remaimng steps. (See Regulations 18702-18708.)


File No. A-01-209
Page No. 3
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
Williams, Jr
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
WLW:jg
